ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Rooh Afza herbal syrup concentrate; closest defensible match is fruit syrup food product (FOODON_03301710). Rooh Afza is a multi-ingredient herbal and floral concentrate, not a pure fruit syrup.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Syrups and similar products) is the closest defensible category for a concentrated herbal-floral syrup. No exact FoodEx2 entry exists for Rooh Afza.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No defensible SNOMED CT code identified for this traditional herbal syrup concentrate.

main_ingredients: permitted food colour (Red No. 40 or equivalent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Rooh Afza is approximately 30 ml (about 2 tablespoons) of the concentrated syrup, typically diluted in 200–250 ml of chilled water or milk before consumption.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), ICMR, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Rooh Afza is not individually listed in the NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods table. Values are adapted from the NIN entry for sugar-based syrups and concentrated sweet beverages, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Syrups, table blends, pancake' (FDC ID 169661) as a secondary anchor for macro validation. The input record values (87.5 g carbohydrate, 350 kcal per 100 g) are consistent with a high-sucrose syrup concentrate and are retained with medium confidence. Protein and fat are confirmed zero by product formulation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — sucrose and high-sucrose syrups
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Rooh Afza in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 75 is estimated by analogy with sucrose (GI ~65–70) and high-sucrose sugar syrups (GI ~68–80) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Rooh Afza contains approximately 85 g sugar per 100 g (predominantly sucrose), supporting a high GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (75 × 26.25 g available carbohydrate per 30 g serving) / 100 = 19.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (15 mg per 100 g); fat-free formulation
hypertension.guidance: Rooh Afza is very low in sodium and contains no fat, making it unlikely to directly elevate blood pressure; however, its very high sugar content (85 g per 100 g) may contribute to weight gain and metabolic stress if consumed in excess, which are indirect risk factors for hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high simple sugar content (85 g per 100 g, predominantly sucrose); high glycemic index (~75)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should strictly limit or avoid Rooh Afza, as its very high sucrose concentration and high glycemic index (~75) will cause rapid and significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; a standard 30 ml serving delivers approximately 25.5 g of sugar and a glycemic load of approximately 19–20.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar intake linked to elevated triglycerides, visceral adiposity, and dyslipidaemia
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of Rooh Afza in undiluted or large quantities is not recommended for individuals with cardiovascular disease, as the high free sugar content (85 g per 100 g) can raise serum triglycerides and contribute to visceral fat accumulation, both established cardiovascular risk factors; occasional use in small diluted servings is lower risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar intake is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Rooh Afza consumption, as high refined sugar intake is associated with increased systemic inflammation and may exacerbate joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rooh Afza is a legendary Indian herbal syrup concentrate first formulated in 1906 by Hakim Hafiz Abdul Majeed in Old Delhi and produced by Hamdard Laboratories. Its name translates from Urdu as 'Soul Refresher', reflecting its traditional role as a potent cooling beverage for hot climates. The syrup is a deep crimson colour with an intensely floral, rose-forward fragrance and a sweet, complex taste derived from a proprietary blend of rose extract, screwpine (kewra), pineapple, orange, spinach, carrot, and various cooling herbs and roots, all suspended in a high-sucrose base. Per 100 g of undiluted syrup, Rooh Afza provides approximately 350 kcal, 87.5 g carbohydrate (of which 85 g is sugar), and negligible protein and fat. It is consumed by diluting 30 ml of syrup in 200–250 ml of chilled water, milk, or carbonated soda. It is a central component of Falooda (a layered dessert drink) and is used as a topping for kulfi and ice cream. Rooh Afza is the most iconic beverage for Iftar (breaking the fast) during Ramadan across the Indian subcontinent, Pakistan, and Bangladesh. Clinically, it is high-glycaemic (GI ~75) and should be strictly avoided by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. Its very low sodium content makes it benign for blood pressure when consumed in moderation, but its high free sugar load poses cardiovascular and metabolic risks with regular or large-volume consumption. It is vegetarian, vegan, and halal. The syrup is shelf-stable and requires no cooking.

meal_context: topping for kulfi and ice cream

confidence_reason: Rooh Afza is not individually listed in the NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central databases. Nutritional values are adapted from NIN sugar-syrup category entries and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central syrup entries. The macro profile (87.5 g carbohydrate, 350 kcal, 0 g protein, 0 g fat per 100 g) is highly consistent with a high-sucrose concentrate and is retained with medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate based on sucrose and high-sucrose syrup data from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: protein_g is confirmed zero — Rooh Afza is a sugar-and-herbal-extract concentrate containing no protein by formulation; this is consistent with USDA FoodData Central syrup category entries. fat_g is confirmed zero — the product contains no fat-bearing ingredients by formulation; saturated, monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fats are all zero by product formulation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero — the filtration and concentration process removes all fibre from the herbal and fruit extracts. vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg are confirmed zero — these are animal-derived or UV-synthesised nutrients absent in a plant-based herbal syrup. 2. GLYCEMIC LOAD CALCULATION: GL per 30 g serving = (GI 75 × available carbohydrate 26.25 g) / 100 = 19.7. Reported as approximately 19.7 per standard 30 ml serving of undiluted syrup. 3. SOURCE ADAPTATION: No exact entry for Rooh Afza exists in NIN or USDA databases. Values adapted from NIN sugar syrup category and USDA FoodData Central 'Syrups, table blends' (FDC ID 169661) as the closest approved-source analogues. 4. PORTIONS: The input record contained four identical duplicate portion entries; deduplicated to one canonical 30 ml serving. 5. FOOD GROUP CORRECTION: Input classified Rooh Afza under 'Snacks'; reclassified to 'Beverages' as it is a liquid syrup concentrate consumed as a drink. 6. WATER CONTENT: water_g estimated at 12.5 g per 100 g (100 − 87.5 g carbohydrate = 12.5 g residual, consistent with a thick syrup concentrate). 7. ONTOLOGY: No exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for Rooh Afza; closest_parent matches documented with gap notes. foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent' per allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].
